nativeRange.habitat: Semi-protected inshore reefs with rich soft and hard coral growth in the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden, south to Zanzibar. Typically encountered between roughly 3 and 30 m depth.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding largely on sponges and tunicates, along with algae and small invertebrates. In captivity tends to do well on a varied diet of sponge-based angelfish preparations, marine algae and seaweed sheets, mysis shrimp, and small chopped meaty foods. Frequent small feedings (2–3 times per day) with access to mature live rock for grazing are generally advised.

summary: A large, striking Red Sea angelfish with a vivid yellow crescent across a dark blue body. Generally considered intermediate in difficulty, semi-aggressive, and best kept singly in a large, mature reef-adjacent system.
careNotes: The Asfur Angelfish (also widely traded as the Arabian Angelfish and occasionally Crescent or Half-Moon Angelfish) is a large Pomacanthus species from the Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, and East African coast south to Zanzibar. LiveAquaria rates the species as easy, while broader hobby coverage tends to treat it as a more demanding large-angel project that needs a mature tank and varied diet; an intermediate care level is documented here. Most sources recommend at least 150–200 US gallons (~570–760 L); the larger end is generally preferred to give adults adequate swimming space, so a minimum of around 800 L (about 210 gal) is used here. Temperature recommendations vary across sources — LiveAquaria suggests roughly 22–26°C while FishBase reports wild ranges of 22–27°C and a preferred range closer to 25–29°C; an averaged working range of approximately 23–27°C is used. Specific gravity is generally kept around 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12. Like other large Pomacanthus angelfishes, this species is a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite and shows a dramatic juvenile-to-adult color change: juveniles display dark blue/black bodies crossed by curved white and pale-blue bars, transitioning to the adult pattern of a dark blue/purple body, black face, broad yellow crescent across the midbody, and yellow caudal fin. Adults tend to be very aggressive toward conspecifics unless introduced as a bonded pair, and are best kept as the only angelfish in a system. The species is generally not considered fully reef-safe — it has been known to nip at stony and soft corals and clam mantles, though some keepers report success with noxious soft corals and SPS. Large angels of this kind tend to be susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when fed a poor diet or kept in marginal water quality, so frequent feedings with marine algae, vitamin-soaked foods, and stable water chemistry are generally advised. Lifespan is not well documented for this species specifically; large Pomacanthus angels have been reported to live around 15–21 years in well-maintained aquariums.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has been reported as extremely rare and is generally limited to commercial aquaculture programs (e.g., captive-bred juveniles occasionally appear on the trade). In the wild this species spawns pelagically as a protogynous hermaphrodite. No reliable home-aquarium breeding protocol is documented in the hobby literature.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacanthidae, Acanthuriformes), max size (40 cm TL), native range (Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, south to Zanzibar), depth (3–30 m), and temperature (22–27°C, preferred 25–29.2°C). Higher than LiveAquaria's 22.2–25.6°C; averaged to 23–27°C. Confirmed wild diet (sponges, tunicates) and strong conspecific aggression.

additional.name: Reef2Reef wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary source returned HTTP 403; replaced by FishBase. Hobby reviews surfaced via web search (reefs.com, AquariumDomain, reefapp.net) consistently recommended 180–200 gal minimum, semi-aggressive temperament, reef-safe-with-caution status, and confirmed conspecific aggression. Tank size biased to the larger end of that spread (800 L).

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner in the wild; captive breeding has been achieved only rarely and in commercial aquaculture settings. No reliable home-aquarium protocol exists.
